coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and structural elucidation of a unique turn-off fluorescent sensor based on oxo-bridged tin (IV) cluster for selective detection of dopamine in biological fluids
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kouser, Robina
Yasir Khan, Huzaifa
Arjmand, Farukh
Tabassum, Sartaj
coredata.dc:description: An oxo-bridged Sn (IV) Cluster, (TOC) was synthesized and fully characterized by FT-IR, UV–vis, 1H NMR, 119Sn NMR, Mass spectrometry and single crystal X-ray diffraction studies. The single-crystal X-ray analysis revealed that the crystal crystallizes in the monoclinic crystal system possessing the P 21/c space group and exhibited a distorted trigonal bipyramidal geometry. The TOC exhibited a unique turn-off fluorescence response for the selective detection of dopamine (DA) over other analytes. The stoichiometry between the TOC and DA was calculated using Job’s plot. The value of the detection limit was found to be 1.33 µM. The Hirshfeld surface analysis was carried out on the crystal structure to investigate the H-H, Cl-H, Cl-Cl, Sn-Cl and Cl-C interaction studies in the molecule. Density Functional Theory (DFT) studies further supported the sensing mechanism, which closely agreed with the experimental results. Furthermore, the TOC chemosensor was used to detect DA in human blood plasma, and molecular docking studies validated the interaction between the chemosensor and protein. Confocal fluorescence imaging studies were carried out and validated TOC sensing ability for DA in human blood plasma.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of DA in human plasma

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Aligarh Muslim University Department of Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University for providing the facilities. The authors would like to express their gratitude to SAIF, Punjab University, Chandigarh for ESI-MS facility. The authors thank the Department of Pathology, JNMCH, AMU, Aligarh for providing human blood plasma. The authors also thank (USIF) AMU for Single crystal X-ray analysis, NMR, SEM and Confocal imaging facilities. The author (Robina Kouser) is gratefully acknowledged to MOTA for financial support (Award no. 201920-NFST-JAM-02540 from Shastri Bhawan, New Delhi).

We gratefully acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University for providing the facilities. The authors would like to express their gratitude to SAIF, Punjab University, Chandigarh for ESI-MS facility. The authors thank the Department of Pathology, JNMCH, AMU, Aligarh for providing human blood plasma. The authors also thank (USIF) AMU for Single crystal X-ray analysis, NMR, SEM and Confocal imaging facilities. The author (Robina Kouser) is gratefully acknowledged to MOTA for financial support (Award no. 201920-NFST-JAM-02540 from Shastri Bhawan, New Delhi).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: ORTEP structure of TOC with labelled atoms.
Absorbance response of TOC with different analytes viz. L-Arg, L-Asc, Azi, L-Glu, L-Tryp, L-Leu, DA, BS, L-valine and Ure in CH3OH: H2O (1: 1 v/v) system.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Oxo- bridged tin(IV)cluster (TOC) upon titration with various analytes and (b) Changes in the emission spectrum of TOC with increasing concentration of DA (0–140 µM) in (1: 1 v/v) CH3OH: H2O system.

The competitive titration experiment of TOC with DA in the presence of different analytes. The blue bar represents the fluorescence intensity of TOC with different analytes. The red bar represents the fluorescence intensity of TOC and DA in the presence of other interfering analytes.
SEM images of (a) TOC (b) TOC + DA and EDX images of (c) TOC (d) TOC + DA.
Molecular docking poses of Oxo- bridged tin (IV) cluster TOC + DA chemosensor with HSA.
Fluorescence titration of TOC with DA (10 – 110 µM) in human blood plasma.
Confocal fluorescence images of (a) TOC, (b) TOC + DA, (c) TOC + H.B.P + DA (10 µM), (D) TOC + H.B.P + DA (20 µM), (E) TOC + H.B.P + DA (30 µM), (F) TOC + H.B.P + DA (40 µM).
Frontier molecular orbitals depicting HOMO and LUMO diagram of TOC and TOC + DA.
Synthesis of Oxo- bridged tin (IV) cluster (TOC).
Crystallographic and structure refinement data of TOC.
Energy values of HOMO LUMO and electronic properties of TOC and TOC + DA.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and structural elucidation of a unique turn-off fluorescent sensor based on oxo-bridged tin (IV) cluster for selective detection of dopamine in biological fluids

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, Uttar Pradesh 202002, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University, Aligarh, Uttar Pradesh 202002, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A unique TOC chemosensor for fluorescence sensing of dopamine in human blood plasma.

An oxo-bridged Sn (IV) Cluster, (TOC) was synthesized and fully characterized by FT-IR, UV–vis, 1H NMR, 119Sn NMR, Mass spectrometry and single crystal X-ray diffraction studies. The single-crystal X-ray analysis revealed that the crystal crystallizes in the monoclinic crystal system possessing the P 21/c space group and exhibited a distorted trigonal bipyramidal geometry. The TOC exhibited a unique turn-off fluorescence response for the selective detection of dopamine (DA) over other analytes. The stoichiometry between the TOC and DA was calculated using Job’s plot. The value of the detection limit was found to be 1.33 µM. The Hirshfeld surface analysis was carried out on the crystal structure to investigate the H-H, Cl-H, Cl-Cl, Sn-Cl and Cl-C interaction studies in the molecule. Density Functional Theory (DFT) studies further supported the sensing mechanism, which closely agreed with the experimental results. Furthermore, the TOC chemosensor was used to detect DA in human blood plasma, and molecular docking studies validated the interaction between the chemosensor and protein. Confocal fluorescence imaging studies were carried out and validated TOC sensing ability for DA in human blood plasma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is a critical neurotransmitter in the phenethylamine and catecholamine families, delivered by neurons, and is present mainly in the central nervous system and mammalian brain [1–4]. It plays a significant role in various functions such as endocrine regulation, prolactin production inhibition, learning, emotion, motivation, and memory and transmits various neurological signals [5,6]. The abnormal DA concentration in the human brain and body may result in various diseases, including schizophrenia, alzheimer’s, huntington’s, parkinson’s, ekbom’s syndrome, neuroblastoma, depression, and cancerous tumours [7–11]. The accurate and ultrasensitive monitoring of DA levels in various biological samples in real-time can change and improve the quality of a patient's life while also lowering treatment costs [12]. Therefore, it is crucial to determine DA concentrations for clinical diagnosis and biomedical research.

Several techniques and methodologies have been developed for the sensing of DA, such as Phung et al. developed Au/Ag nanocluster for sensing DA by surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy method [13]. Li et al. developed a boron-doped diamond electrode for selective sensing of DA by cyclic voltammetry method [14]. Jiang et al. developed an N-doped carbon dots for DA sensing by electrochemical method [15]. However, these techniques have numerous disadvantages. For example, the chromatographic methods require expensive equipment and cumbersomesample pre-treatment protocols; electrochemical approaches are frequently interferedby ascorbic acid and uric acid compounds due to their comparableoxidation potential values; and the spectrophotometric methodology has considerably low sensitivity [16–20]. Such inherent flaws render these techniques challenging to meet the increasing demand for easy, cost-effective and reliable methods for DA detection. Fluorescent methods on the other hand, have attracted more attention for detecting DA due to their high sensitivity, good reproducibility, fast response, effortless control, cost effectiveness, and excellent selectivity. Therefore, it is considered as a substitute method with great potential.

Furthermore, fluorescent methods have the advantage of sensing DA in complicated matrices without the necessity of separation [21,22]. Chemosensors' proficiency in bonding to neurotransmitters, especially catecholamines, has attracted more attention. Recently, many researchers have focused on detecting DA using the fluorometric approach. Some noteworthy reports are research works of He et al. fabricated a CDots – AuNCs for highly selective sensing of DA [23]; Zhao et al. synthesized a quantum dot for quantitative detection of DA [24]; Ling et al. developed a lanthanide doped nanoparticle for DA detection [25]; AlQarni et al. designed a nitrogen-doped QD for turn-on sensing of DA [26]; while Mu et al. developed adenosine-capped QDs for fluorescent sensing of DA [27]. The fluorescence sensors previously reported have been synthesizedby laborious synthesis procedures, and multiple materials were required for their preparation. Thus, it was thought worthwhile to develop a single fluorescent material using a simple synthetic approach that significantly simplifies the operation and lowers the cost for selective sensing of DA.

As a novel class ofcrystalline substances, cluster compounds have received a lot of attention due to their tunability, fascinating structural diversity, and unusual properties such as more significant surface area, high chemical, and thermal stability, and elastic surface properties [28–31]. Additionally, they can be dissolved in common solvents while maintaining theirstructural integrity. Metal clusters have shown wide applications in catalysis, drug delivery, gas storage, chemosensing, and bioimaging [25,32–34]. Compared to the conventional fluorophores, viz. organic moieties, nanoparticles and quantum dots, metal clusters have superior properties due to excellent biocompatibility and higher fluorescence. Organotin clustersare widely used as surface curing agents, anti-cancer agents,catalysts, chemosensing, andindustrial biocides asthey are stable in air and moisture [35]. Organotin (IV) complexes,in particular,have been intensively studied due to their diversity in structure, catalysis, biological activity, and wide industrial and agricultural applications [36]. Tin (IV) compounds have also become an important class of compounds in supramolecular chemistry for designing receptor for selective recognition of ions or neutral molecules [37]. Klaus Jurkschat and his group were the pioneers in this field, developing organotin (IV) halides for selective sensing of anions [38]. Kumar et al designed organotin (IV) derivatives for efficient detection of permanganate ions [39]. However, sensing DA neurotransmitters by employing an oxo-bridged-based tin (IV) cluster was not reported previously to best of our knowledge.

In this manuscript, we have reported a tin (IV) oxo bridged cluster-dimeric dichlorotertamethyldistannoxane (TOC) for the fluorescence sensing of DA. The TOC exhibited a unique and selective turn-off fluorescence sensing for DA over other analytes. TOC was characterized by single X-ray diffraction studies and other techniques viz., FT-IR, UV–vis, 1H NMR, 119Sn NMR, and Mass spectrometry. The density functional theory calculations were further determined to support the experimental studies. The proposed approach was alsohighly successful in detecting DA in human blood plasma. The synthesized TOC chemosensor for sensing DA was compared to the previously described chemosensor in literature. It was shown that the TOC chemosensor exhibited better and more enhanced performance in terms of the detection limit. We are reporting for the first time the turn-off fluorescence oxo-bridged tin cluster for DA detection in biological samples.

All of the chemicals were of analytical gradeand could be used without prior purification—the dimethyltin dichloride and other analytes. L-arginine (L-Arg), L-ascorbic acid (L-Asc), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-tryptophan (L-Tryp), L-leucine (L-Leu), L-valine (L-Val), urea (Ure) dopamine hydrochloride (DA), azithromycin (Azi), and benzene sulphonamide (BS) were obtained from sigma Aldrich. All the solvents are purchased from Merck. The Human blood plasma was taken from the Pathology Department, JNMC, A.M.U Aligarh. A Perkin Elmer FT-IR spectrometer wasused to measure the IR spectra employingKBr pellets across 4000 to 400 cm−1. The UV–vis studies were determined by using Shimadzu UV-1700 spectrometer. The fluorescence studies were performed on a Shimadzu F-2700 FL Spectrophotometer using a quartz cuvette with a 1 cm path length and a 5 nm slit width. The high-resolution mass spectra were measured using an Agilent ESI-Q-TOF mass spectrometer. The 1H NMR spectra in DMSO‑d 6 were recorded on JEOL 400 MHz spectrometer. A Bruker SMART APEX diffractometer was used to determine the single crystal X-ray data. SEM images were acquired with ascanning electron JEOL JSM-6510LVmicroscope. The confocal images were taken by laser scanning Zeiss LSM 780 confocal microscope.

The TOC was synthesized by the slight modification of the method reported by R. Graziani et al. using a conventional reaction of dimethyltin dichloride in a mixed solution of dichloromethane and methanol under stirring conditions for 6 hr [40]. To a hot and stirred methanolic (5 mL) solution of dimethyltin dichloride (0.219 g) was added dropwise a dichloromethane (3 mL) solution. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6 h. The resulted white powder was then filtered and recrystallized from a CH2Cl2 - hexane mixture. After two days, colourlesscrystals were obtained. Subsequently, the TOC was characterized by various techniques viz. single X-ray diffraction studies, FT-IR, 1H NMR, 119Sn NMR, and Mass spectrometry [C8H24Cl4O2Sn4], Yield: 68%, Melting point: 180 °C, 119Sn NMR (500 MHz, DMSO, ppm): − 158.53, − 134.92, 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO, ppm): δ 2.50 (–CH3), δ 3.37 (–CH3), FTIR (KBr cm−1): 2918 ѵ(C–H), 791 ѵ(Sn-C), 553(Sn-O), ESI-MS calculated for one unit of TOC = 364.50 and found = 364.89.

The stock solution of TOC was prepared in methanolic solution at 1.0 × 10-3 mol/L concentration which was further diluted to a concentration of at 1.0 × 10-6 mol/L. The stock solution of various analytes viz. (L-Arg), (L-Asc), (L-Glu), (L-Tryp), (L-Leu), (L-Val), (Ure) (DA), (Azi), and (BS) were prepared in distilled water at a concentration of 1.0 × 10-3 mol/L which was further diluted to a concentration of 1.0 × 10-5 mol/L.

Using UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy, the photophysical studies of TOC with different analytes in CH3OH: H2O solution at 1:1 v/v system were performed. The fluorescence measurements were performed using an excitation wavelength of 253 nm and a slit width of 5 nm. All the spectral measurements were performed at room temperature.

The DFT calculations were performed using the ORCA computing tool with the def2-TZVP basis set and B3LYP functional [41]. Avogadro's programme estimates the HOMO and LUMO energies of the optimised structures [42].

The oxo- bridged tin (IV) cluster (TOC) was synthesized by a reaction of dimethyltin dichloride in a mixed solution of methanol and dichloromethane (Scheme 1 ) The structure of TOC was validated by employing single X-ray diffraction studies and various other spectroscopic techniques viz., FT-IR, 1H NMR, 119 Sn NMR, and mass spectroscopy (Figs. S1- S4). The colourless crystals of TOC were soluble in different organic solvents such as methanol, ethanol, and dimethyl sulfoxide. The sensing properties of TOC to various analyteswere studied in a CH3OH: H2O system.

The proposed structure of TOCwas confirmed by employingsingle-crystal X-ray diffraction studies. The ORTEP (Oak Ridge Thermal Ellipsoid Plot Program) structure of TOC is portrayed in Fig. 1 . The single-crystal X-ray analysis revealed that the compound TOC crystallizes in the monoclinic crystal system possessing the P 21/c space group and the lattice parameters of, a = 7.1062(3)Å, b = 18.1989(7)Å, c = 8.6749(3)Å, α = γ = 90°, β = 109.424(1) and Z = 4 per unit cell. The crystallographic data and structure refinement parameters are summarized in Table 1 . The selected bond length and bond angles are depicted in Table S1. The TOCconsists of a central four-membered Sn2O2 ring, and two subsidiaries of four-membered rings Sn2OCl have connected adjacently. The Sn, O and Cl atoms of the TOCare nearly coplanar.

The trigonality index, τ = (β-α)/ 60 [where β and α are two largest basal angles] is an important parameter to determine the geometry of five co-ordinated metal. The τ value around Sn1 and Sn2 centre was found to be 0.84 and 0.89 respectively, which are close to the angle present in distorted trigonal bipyramidal geometry [43]. Therefore, TOCexhibited a distorted trigonal bipyramidal geometry around both the endocyclic and exocyclic tin centre. The value of bond distance of Sn(1) - O(1) and Sn(2) - O(1) are 2.05 and 2.03 respectively. Furthermore, the stacking interaction in the molecule strengthen the supramolecular architecture and molecular framework in crystal lattices. The intermolecular interactionsand –CH -Cl interactions in the crystal lattice furtherreinforce the stability of the crystal structure of TOC. In the solid state, the TOCexhibits many crucial noncovalent interactions to form a lattice shown in Fig. S5a. The TOChas a unique staircase-like packing alignment. The molecular packing viewed is also shown in Fig. S5b.

The mapping of the Hirshfeld surface in a crystal molecule displays three-dimensional electron density, which provides qualitative and quantitative insight into the strong intermolecular interactions surrounding the molecule [44]. The Hirshfeld surfaces and 2D fingerprint plots were created using the crystal explorer software. As shown in Fig. S6. the HFSA shows various types of plots such as dnorm, shape index and shape curvedness. The dnorm surfaceemphasizes the interactions of atoms with lengths equal to the total of their van der Waals radii. The red spots on the dnorm surfaces indicated the presence of close contacts in terms of hydrogen bonding interactions, which are mainly involved in the molecular packing of the crystal structure of TOC. The complementary regions with red-blue triangles on the shape index maps illustrated the interplanar stacking interactions. Thecurvedness is used to visualize the planar stacking alignment and interaction with the adjacent molecule. The 2D fingerprint plots of TOCwere also generated for quantifying intermolecular interactions, which were then fragmented to study per-atom interactions inside the molecule (Fig. S7). The fingerprint plots showed various types ofsignificant contributions such as H-H, Cl-H, Cl-Cl, Sn-Cl and Cl-C. The H-H interactions were the dominant interactions, accounting for approximately 69.5% of the total Hirshfeld surface. The second-highest interactions involved in crystal packing were discovered to be Cl-H. The Cl-Cl interactions contributed 2.2% of the total surface area. In contrast, interactions caused by the Sn-Cl and Cl-C atoms were sparse, accounting for only 1.9% and 0.1% of the total intermolecular interactions in the crystal structure.

UV–vis spectroscopy was used to investigate the TOC chemosensor optical properties with various analytes, L-Arg, L-Asc, L-Glu, L-Tryp, L-Leu, L-Val, Ure, DA, Azi, and BS. The sensing performance of the TOCchemosensor was studied using absorption measurements in a CH3OH: H2O (1: 1 v/v) system. As shown in Fig. 2 upon addition of one equivalent of DA to the TOC solution (10 µM), a significant bathochromic shift of 34 nm was observed in the absorption spectra, and the peak intensity also increased. However, minor changes were observed when the same concentration of other analytes ware added to the TOC solution. These substantial changes in the spectra could be employed for selective sensing of DA in the presence of other analytes and validate that TOC is predominantly specific for sensing DA.

The fluorescence emission studies of TOC were recorded with different analytes viz. L-Arg, L-Asc, L-Glu, L-Tryp, L-Leu, L-Val, Ure, DA, Azi, and BS in CH3OH: H2O (1: 1 v/v) system. The fluorescence spectra of TOC (10 µM) emitted higher fluorescence intensity at 306 nm upon excitation at a wavelength of 253 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 a, upon adding DA to the TOC solution, the fluorescence intensity quenched prominently up to many folds. While only slight changes were observed when the same concentration of other analytes were added to the TOC solution, donating that the TOC can serve as a highly effective fluorescent chemosensor for sensing of DA. Furthermore, to evaluate the sensitivity of TOC towards DA, the fluorescence spectra were recorded with the progressive addition of DA in the range of 0 – 140 µM (Fig. 3b). The spectra indicate that increasing DA concentration has a quenching effect on TOC fluorescence, resulting in a substantialdecrease in emission intensity which further depicts that fluorescence intensity of TOC was effectively dependent on DA concentration. Therefore, the results suggested that the TOC could be used as an effective fluorescent chemosensor for turn off sensing of DA.

High selectivity is a prerequisite for an excellent chemosensor. A competitive titration experiment was carried out to validate the proposed method with different analytes, such as L-Arg, L-Asc, L-Glu, L-Tryp, L-Leu, L-Val, Ure, DA, Azi, and BS. The fluorescence change of TOC (10 µM) was measured by adding one equivalent amount of DA in the presence of the same concentration of other interfering analytes (10 µM). As shown in Fig. 4
, the competitive analytes do not cause any significant variation in the sensing of DA. Therefore, these findings demonstrated that the TOC could efficiently detectDA, thusmaking it a highly selective and robust chemosensor for DA detection.

The binding stoichiometry betweenTOC andDAwas calculated using Job's plot method [45]. The overall concentration of TOCand DAremained constant at 100 µM while the mole ratio of DAvaried from 0.1 to 0.9. As shown in Fig. S8, the job's plot shows a maximum at a mole ratio of 0.7, indicating that the binding ratio between DAand TOCwas 2:1.Moreover, the detection limit of TOCfor DAwas estimated to be 1.33 µM using the equation 3 σ /k, where σ and K stand for standard deviation and slope of the linear calibration curve, respectively (Fig. S9).

We have explored the potential sensing mechanism that causes quenching in the fluorescence intensity of TOC by DA. As shown in Fig. S10, a significant spectral overlap occurs between the absorption spectrum of DA and the excitation spectrum of TOC. This overlap suggests that a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism occurs between DA and TOC, which further results in quenching in the fluorescence intensity of TOC [1,46]. Furthermore, the interactions between the TOC and DA were explored by FT-IR and 1H NMR studies. Upon adding one equivalent of DA to TOC, a moderate shift in the IR bands of TOC was observed at 791 cm−1 and 553 cm−1, and new peaks of DA also appeared (Fig. S11). In the case of 1H NMR, an up-field shift in the –CH3 protons of TOC, and significant peaks of DA were also observed in Fig. S12. This further supports the interaction between TOC and DA. The probable binding mode between TOC and DA is displayed in Scheme S1.

Scanning electron microscopy images were used to assess the morphological investigations of TOC with DA. As shown in Fig. 5a the TOC showed a crystal shape random morphology. However, upon addition of DA to TOC, the SEM images show an irregular framework, such as the size of TOC + DA becomes compact and forms an agglomerate molecule due to the interaction between TOC and DA (Fig. 5b). Furthermore, to determine the elemental composition, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy was employed. The EDX result demonstrates the presence of carbon, oxygen, tin and chlorine in TOC (Fig. 5c) and carbon, oxygen, tin, chlorine and nitrogen in TOC + DA (Fig. 5d), which further evidence the successful binding of TOC with DA.

Molecular docking is a vital tool for determining the chemosensor and protein interaction. Docking studies between the DA, HSA (PDB ID: 1A06), and TOC were carried out using Hex software. Human Serum Albumin (HSA) is the most significant and abundant extracellular protein in human blood plasma, accounting for approximately 60% of total plasma proteins (out of 9% proteins) in plasma. As shown in Fig. 6 the TOC + DA show binding affinity with Asp-121 and Glu-119 amino acids. DA + HSA has a binding energy of −174.44 kJ/mol. Moreover, TOC docked with DA + HSA has a binding energy of – 434.03 KJ/mol, which implicated stronger binding affinity of TOC with DA + HSA. Various hydrophobic, charge, and hydrogen bonding interactions with some residues of HSA protein surround the TOC + DA molecule.

Furthermore, because of the globular domain structural arrangements of HSA, it offers a diversity of binding sites for different chemosensors. It is a key factor in determining the pharmacokinetic behaviour of many drugs [47]. As shown in Fig. 7 upon the increasing concentration of DA to TOC in a plasma medium, there is a decrease in fluorescence intensity. Thus, fluorescence quenching of TOC in a plasma medium may be due to the binding of TOC with HSA proteins in presence of DA. The binding mode of TOC with DA and HSA is shown in Fig. S13. Moreover, numerous findings reveal quenching of the intrinsic fluorescence of HSA upon interaction with different molecules [48]. Thus, the results depict that DA and TOC binds to HSA, which is a prominent drug carrier protein in human blood plasma.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of DA in human plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To further determine the practical utility of TOC for detecting DA, fluorescence studies were performed in human blood plasma [49]. The objective was to evaluate versatility of TOC and potential applicability for DA detection in human plasma. As shown in Fig. 7, the fluorescence intensity of TOC decreases with the addition of DA in human plasma. The titration experiment of TOC was carried out in human blood plasma with the addition of DA in the range of 10 – 110 µM. A gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed. A similar trend in a decrease in fluorescence intensity was obtained from an aqueous medium (Fig. 3b). Considering all these findings, the proposed method could be used to detect DA in actual blood samples, with the potential to be extended to the diagnosis and treatment of disorders connected to the central nervous system.

Given the favourable findings of the spectrofluorometric analysis, we endured in conducting confocal fluorescence imaging investigations to investigate TOC sensing ability to detect DA in human blood serum. As presented in Fig. 8 a the TOC shows bright fluorescence images. However, upon adding an equivalent concentration of DA to TOC solution, a reduction in fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 8b). Similar results were obtained from Fig. 3a. Furthermore, DA sensing was performed in plasma medium (Fig. 8c-f). The bright fluorescence of the TOC in human blood plasma (H.B.P) significantly diminished after the incremental addition of DA. The fluorescence quenching became noticeable after increasing the DA concentration to 40 µM. Thus, these findings demonstrated that the as-synthesized TOC could be an excellent chemosensor for biological and chemical sensing applications.

The TOC chemosensor was compared to previously described chemosensors. In comparison to the reported chemosensors, the TOC exhibited a lower detection limit value for DA, as presented in Table 3 . As a result, the synthesized TOC is better than other sensors in terms of detection limit and could be employed as a steady chemosensor for detecting DA in various biological and environmental samples.

In this manuscript, we have reported an Oxo- bridged tin(IV)cluster (TOC) for the fluorescence sensing of DA. TOC was thoroughly characterized by FT-IR, UV–vis, 1H NMR, 119Sn NMR, Mass spectrometry and single X-ray diffraction studies. The Hirschfeld surface analysis was performed on the crystal structure to explore the interaction studies in the TOC. The fingerprint plot depicts that the H-H interactions were the dominant interactions, accounting for approximately 69.5% of the total Hirshfeld surface. The TOC exhibited a unique and selective turn-off fluorescence sensing for DA over other analytes investigated by employing UV–vis and Fluorescence spectroscopic techniques. The competitive experiment revealed that the other interfering analytes do not cause any significant variation in the sensing of DA. The 2:1 stoichiometry between DA and TOC was determined from Jobs plot. The value of the detection limit was found to be 1.33 µM. The scanning electron microscopy images were used to assess the morphological investigations of TOC with DA and the EDX results showed the successful binding of TOC with DA. The reduction in the band gap of TOC-DA in density functional theory calculations is strongly endorsed by the prominent bathochromic shift noticed in the UV–vis absorption spectra upon addition of DA to the TOC. The proposed approach was alsohighly successful in detecting DA in human blood plasma. Moreover, confocal fluorescence imaging studies and molecular docking results further confirm the sensing ability of TOC for detection of DA in human plasma. Thus, TOC could be used as an excellent chemosensor for the selective detection of DA in biological samples.

Robina Kouser: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Huzaifa Yasir Khan: Data curation. Farukh Arjmand: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Sartaj Tabassum: Investigation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, Aligarh Muslim University for providing the facilities. The authors would like to express their gratitude to SAIF, Punjab University, Chandigarh for ESI-MS facility. The authors thank the Department of Pathology, JNMCH, AMU, Aligarh for providing human blood plasma. The authors also thank (USIF) AMU for Single crystal X-ray analysis, NMR, SEM and Confocal imaging facilities. The author (Robina Kouser) is gratefully acknowledged to MOTA for financial support (Award no. 201920-NFST-JAM-02540 from Shastri Bhawan, New Delhi).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123152.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots via hydrothermal synthesis: naked eye fluorescent sensor for dopamine and used for multicolor cell imaging
ACS Appl. Bio Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Naik, P. Zantye, D. Gunjal, A. Gore, P. Anbhule, M. Kowshik, S. V. Bhosale, G. Kolekar, Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots via Hydrothermal Synthesis: Naked Eye Fluorescent Sensor for Dopamine and Used for Multicolor Cell Imaging, ACS Appl. Bio Mater. 2 (2019) 2069–2077. 10.1021/acsabm.9b00101.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A label-free and ultrasensitive fluorescent sensor for dopamine detection based on double-stranded DNA templated copper nanoparticles
Sens. Actuat. B Chem.
